title: Dog Hot and Red Nipples: Nursing Mastitis Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your nursing dog has hot, red nipples, it may be mastitis. Learn about symptoms, urgency levels, and how to help your pet today.
content_html:
Understanding Mastitis in Nursing Dogs

If your nursing dog has nipples that are hot to the touch, red, or swollen, she is likely experiencing mastitis. Mastitis is an infection or inflammation of the mammary glands caused by bacteria entering the teat or by milk backup (galactostasis). It is a painful condition that can make the mother reluctant to feed her puppies and, if left untreated, can lead to serious systemic illness.

Is This an Emergency?

This situation is classified as Medium Urgency. While it may not require an immediate midnight trip to the ER in every case, it does require a veterinary appointment within 12 to 24 hours. If your dog develops a high fever, stops eating, or the nipple appears black or purple, the urgency increases to high, as the infection may have become gangrenous or systemic.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected mammary glands is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A visual assessment allows a professional to check for signs of abscesses, skin necrosis, or significant swelling that might not be fully captured by description alone. This helps the clinic determine how quickly your dog needs to be squeezed into the schedule.

faqs.question: Can the puppies keep nursing if the mother has mastitis?
faqs.answer: In many cases, it is actually beneficial for puppies to continue nursing to help clear the gland, unless the milk is clearly discolored or the mother is in too much pain.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of mastitis in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include firm, swollen mammary glands, redness, heat, pain upon touching, and sometimes a change in the color or consistency of the milk.

faqs.question: How do veterinarians treat mastitis?
faqs.answer: Treatment typically involves a course of dog-safe antibiotics, anti-inflammatory medication for pain, and sometimes warm compresses applied to the affected area.
